summary: Design several elegant light+dark color palettes for the pointillist terrain (parallel designers → judge → concrete hex specs)

palettes.name: Nordic Slate & Fog
palettes.mood: A still fjord morning: desaturated blue-grays, brushed steel, and dots that settle like frost on stone — architectural calm, nothing loud.
palettes.rationale: One hue family throughout: a cool blue-gray (~205-210deg) held at low-to-moderate chroma so it reads as slate and fog, never teal or navy. Dark variant: the sky drops from #1c242c to a near-black #090c10, and the elevation ramp climbs in luminance along that same hue — muted steel valley (#455a68) to lighter steel mid to a bright frost peak (#c3d6df), so ridgelines catch light like rime on rock while low ground recedes. Dot luminance sits well above the deep sky at every elevation, so shape reads without any dot glowing garishly. Light variant inverts the logic: a pale fog sky (#eef1f3 to a cool #b4c1c9) carries deeper slate dots, valley darkest (#37444f) rising to a lighter-but-still-grounded steel peak (#728794) — the ramp stays dark enough against pale fog to hold contrast top-to-bottom. Walkers are the only near-neutrals: a bright frost glow, a cool steel settled tone, and a slate trail — subtle punctuation, same family. Clock ink is a frost near-white (dark) / deep slate (light), each paired with an opposing halo so the time stays legible over both the gradient and the densest dots.

palettes.name: Ember Dusk
palettes.mood: Distant campfire embers glowing across a mountain as the sky burns down to deep plum-charcoal — warm, quiet, the last hour of light.
palettes.rationale: The whole palette lives on a single warm hue arc — plum → rose → terracotta → amber — so nothing feels grafted on. Dark: the sky descends from a dusky plum-charcoal (#2a2030) to a near-black plum (#0a060c), a deep, smooth 6-stop field that reads as night with warmth still in it, never flat black. The elevation ramp is a true dark→light, cool-warm→hot progression: valley is a dim rose-brown ember (#783a34) that barely smolders in the low ground, mid warms to terracotta (#b25c42), and peaks flare to a muted amber (#e69e5c) — bright enough to read as light on the plum sky but held well short of neon orange (chroma is restrained, value tops out around 90%). Because peaks are the warmest AND lightest, the mountain's shape reads instantly. Walkers stay in-family: a warm cream spark (#f5e2c8) for the glow, an amber settle, and a dim rose trail so they feel like drifting cinders, not colored confetti. Light: the sky is a warm cream that ripens downward into dusty mauve-rose (#b8929a), the daytime memory of the same dusk. On that pale field the ramp inverts in value but keeps the hue story — deep plum-brown valley (#5c363a), terracotta mid, warm ochre-amber peak (#c4804a) — all dark enough for strong contrast on cream yet still warm, so peaks again read as the lifted, lighter-warmer element. Clock legibility is covered both ways: dark uses a warm off-white ink (#eee4d6) with a deep-plum halo; light uses a deep plum ink (#2e1e24) with a cream halo, and both ink colors sit far in luminance from every dot color and every sky stop, so the numerals never lose their edge over either the gradient or a cluster of embers.

palettes.name: Sage & Moss
palettes.mood: A quiet botanical morning: moss on stone, olive leaves, and a low thread of gold — earthy, warm-green, and unhurried.
palettes.rationale: The whole palette lives on a single warm-green axis (hue ~70-95, olive to moss) with a low-chroma gold accent, so it reads as one botanical family rather than a rainbow. The elevation ramp climbs cool-and-dark to warm-and-light in both modes — valley is a deep shaded moss, mid an olive leaf, peak a sun-touched gold-sage — giving the terrain an unmistakable light gradient so the ridge shape stays legible. Dark: luminous olive/gold dots (L* ~48 to ~80) on a near-black forest sky (L* ~8-12) clear the ~40+ luminance-contrast bar without glowing neon; the sky's faint green tint ties it to the dots. Light: dots are pushed dark and saturated (valley #556b3b, moss and gold above) against a pale linen-sage sky (L* ~70-95), so each dot sits at least ~30 L* below the background it touches and never washes out. Warmer and greener than Classic teal by design — the mids and peaks carry olive/gold rather than cyan. Clock ink is a deep olive-black on light with a linen halo, and a warm bone-white on dark with a near-black halo; both are neutral-enough to stay readable over sky and over the brightest peak dots.

palettes.name: Sumi-e Ink Wash
palettes.mood: A mountain drawn in a single loaded brush: graphite ridgelines breathing on rice paper by day, silver peaks surfacing from ink-black by night. 山水, held to a whisper.
palettes.rationale: The whole palette lives on one axis — a near-neutral gray with the faintest cool (blue-gray) bias, so nothing ever reads as a hue, only as ink density. LIGHT: the sky opens warm at the top (#f7f6f2, true rice-paper) and cools/deepens toward the base (#cbced5), giving a soft atmospheric recession without ever going dark. Dots run light-to-dark valley→peak (#8b8f96 → #31353d), so the highest ridges carry the strongest graphite and the shape reads as pen-and-ink relief; peak L is ~22 against paper L ~96, ample contrast but never black-on-white harsh. DARK: sky is layered ink-black (#1a1d22 → #050506) with the same cool tint; dots invert to dark-to-light valley→peak (#575d67 → #c6ccd5) so peaks surface as luminous silver — the classic dark-variant "light on deep" model. Walkers are pure value events: a near-white/near-silver glow, a mid graphite settled tone, and a recessive trail, adding sparkle without introducing color. Clock ink sits at the extreme of the value range in each mode (deep ink #23262c on paper, silver #dadde2 on ink) with a shadow drawn straight from the sky's terminal stop, so it stays legible over both the gradient and the densest dots. Coherence comes from every color being a sample of the same monochrome ramp — maximal restraint, expensive quiet.

palettes.name: Aurora Indigo
palettes.mood: A still winter night: an indigo-violet sky with a cool aurora breathing across the ridge in teal, pale cyan, and mint.
palettes.rationale: The whole palette lives on a single cool axis: indigo/violet for the sky, a teal-to-mint aurora for the dots, so every element shares a blue-green undertone and reads as one world. DARK is the hero: the sky descends from a lit violet-indigo (#211b46) to near-black indigo (#060410) in six even stops for a smooth, deep field with no banding. The dots climb teal (valley #2e8b82) to pale cyan (mid #7cd2d8) to luminous mint (peak #b0f0cd) — a clear low-chroma-to-high, dark-to-light progression so elevation reads instantly, and all three sit well above the sky's luminance so the ridge glows without going neon (peaks stay just shy of white). The walker trail (#8f86d6) is a soft violet pulled straight from the sky hue, tying the particles to their background while the teal settled color keeps them in the aurora family. Clock ink is a cool near-white (#e8ecf5) with a deep-indigo halo, legible over both the dark sky and the brightest mint peaks. LIGHT inverts the value relationship without breaking the hue story: a pale lavender-to-periwinkle sky (#f2f1fb → #b9bed9, cool not warm) carries deeper, more saturated dots — deep teal valley to a medium mint peak — so they read as darker marks on a pale ground with ample contrast, peak still the lightest of the three so shape holds. The glow sits near the sky tone as a bright core (matching the Classic behavior), the trail becomes a deeper indigo for contrast, and the clock uses dark indigo ink with a pale halo. Restrained chroma throughout keeps it in fine-data-viz / muted-film territory rather than RGB.

rankings.name: 1. Nordic Slate & Fog
rankings.note: Single blue-gray hue held at low chroma reads as architectural calm; dark ramp climbs L* 37→56→84 and light ramp sits 28→41→55 dark-on-pale, both clearing contrast top-to-bottom; only overlap is with Sumi's grays, but the clear steel chroma keeps it distinct from monochrome and from Classic teal.

rankings.name: 4. Sumi-e Ink Wash
rankings.note: Every color is a sample of one monochrome ramp — maximal restraint, the most 'expensive quiet' of the set and the most on-brand for a quant minimalist; legibility strong (light inverts so peak is darkest), docked only for near-white-clock-over-silver-peak (dark contrast 1.19) and a soft light-valley (L*59 vs paper), and it competes with Nordic for the cool-gray slot.

rankings.name: 2. Ember Dusk
rankings.note: Gorgeous plum→amber arc and the most emotionally distinct mood, but legibility is the weakest: dark valley embers nearly sink into the plum sky (dLtop +18, contrast 1.82) and the light amber peak washes out over the mauve sky bottom (contrast 1.17).

rankings.name: 3. Sage & Moss
rankings.note: Coherent olive→gold botanical family, dark ramp reads cleanly (49→67→82); knocked for a light gold peak that goes thin near the sky's darker base (contrast 1.15) — otherwise a warm, unhurried alternative well clear of Classic teal.

rankings.name: 5. Aurora Indigo
rankings.note: The hero dark variant is beautiful but pushes toward neon (peak L*90, mint near white) and the near-white clock nearly vanishes over the mint peaks (contrast 1.10); worst of all, teal→mint dots on near-black is conceptually the closest of the five to the Classic teal-on-black it's meant to differ from.

judgment.fixes: Ember Dusk (closest fixable): (1) lift the dark valley so embers don't sink into the sky — #783a34 → #8f4940 (raises dot-vs-sky contrast from 1.82 to ~2.4); (2) rescue the light peak that washes out over the sky base — either darken the peak #c4804a → #b8763f, or darken the sky's bottom stop #b8929a → #a8828e so the amber keeps ~2.0 contrast at the base. Aurora Indigo: tame the near-neon/near-white-clock collision by pulling the mint peak #b0f0cd → #8fe0b8 (drops peak L* ~90→~83, and clock-vs-peak contrast rises past 1.3); optionally shift the light-mode trail/valley cooler to lean further from Classic. Sage & Moss: darken the light gold peak #ab9a52 → #948435 (light peak contrast 1.15→~1.6 at sky base) and/or deepen the sky bottom #8b9280 → #7d8574. Nordic (already strong, small margin): nudge the light peak #728794 → #64768 5... i.e. #647685 for extra headroom against the pale fog. Sumi (near-perfect): give the light valley a touch more ink, #8b8f96 → #7f838c, so the lowest dots don't fade on rice-paper (contrast 3.0→~3.6).
